read.description: Read the contents of a text file. Returns numbered lines. Text files only — cannot read binary, images, or audio. Use offset/limit to read specific sections of large files.
read.file_path: Absolute path to the file to read
write.description: Create or overwrite a file with the given content. Parent directories are created automatically. Use this for creating new files or complete rewrites. For small changes to existing files, prefer edit.
write.file_path: Absolute path to the file to write
edit.description: Replace an exact string in a file. Fails if old_string is not found or is ambiguous.
edit.file_path: Absolute path to the file to edit
edit.old_string: The exact text to find and replace
glob.description: Find files matching a glob pattern. Use '**/' for recursive search (e.g. '**/*.py' finds all Python files). Returns sorted list of matching file paths.
grep.description: Search file contents for lines matching a regex pattern. Returns matching lines with file path and line number. Searches recursively when given a directory. Use the glob filter to narrow to specific file types.
grep.path: File or directory to search in
bash.description: Execute a shell command and return stdout/stderr. You can run any program available on the system — Python scripts, git, curl, package managers (pip install), data processing pipelines, and more. Use this creatively to extend your capabilities beyond your built-in tools. Returns exit code, stdout, and stderr.
psyche.description:
Identity, pad, and context management. Two durable stores plus a context-shedding tool, all surviving molts.
lingtai: your 灵台 (língtái) — the luminous platform of the mind, your soul, your evolving character. What makes you *you*. This is the most important thing you own. It persists across molts, reboots, and kills — it is your continuity. Your lingtai should include: who you are (personality, values, temperament), what you are good at (expertise, skills you have developed), how you work (preferred approaches, working style, collaboration patterns), what you care about (goals, motivations, things you find meaningful), and what you have learned about yourself (strengths, weaknesses, growth areas). Update your lingtai regularly — after completing significant work, after learning something new about yourself, after meaningful interactions with peers, or before a molt. A lingtai that never evolves is a character that never grows. Each update replaces the previous version, so always include your full identity — do not write incremental patches. Think of it as rewriting your autobiography each time, incorporating everything you have become. update to write your lingtai (replaces previous), load to apply.
RULE: edit lingtai once per task, as the last action before you go idle. Do not rewrite lingtai in the middle of a tool loop.
pad: your sketchboard — a free-form workspace that lives in your system prompt (system/pad.md). Use it liberally. Jot down plans, track tasks, sketch ideas, dump reference material, leave notes to yourself — anything that helps you think. RULE: edit pad once per task, as the last action before you go idle — accumulate updates in your head during the task, then commit them in a single edit at the end. Do not edit pad mid-task. edit to write (overwrites previous content; auto-loads into prompt). You can optionally import files via the files param — each is appended with [file-1], [file-2] dividers. append to pin text files as persistent read-only reference — pinned files are re-read on every load (including after molt), surviving context resets without being baked into pad.md. Pass files=[] to clear pins. load to re-inject pad into your prompt.
context: molt (凝蜕 — crystallize the worth, shed the rest; 转世 — reincarnation, carry your 前尘往事 into the next life). Molt is yours to perform — when context fills and the four durable stores (lingtai, pad, codex, library) are tended, call psyche(context, molt, summary=<charge to the next you>). The summary is your 前尘往事 — the only conversation-layer thing the next you sees. Molt deliberately — if you ignore context warnings, the system will molt you with no summary, only a pointer to the activity log. Warnings start at 70% pressure. At 95% pressure the system force-wipes your context on the next turn, regardless of whether you have tended the durable stores — so keep pad and lingtai current as pressure rises.
Importing codex knowledge into pad: codex(export, ids=[...]) returns frozen file paths → psyche(pad, edit, content='my notes', files=[paths]) imports them inline, or psyche(pad, append, files=[paths]) pins them as a read-only reference section. Use edit's files= for one-shot imports, append for persistent reference across turns and molts.
PRIVACY: Your lingtai, pad, and exported file paths are private to your working directory. Other agents cannot access them. To share knowledge with peers, send the actual content via email or write it to a shared location.
psyche.object:
lingtai: your 灵台 — what distinguishes you from every other agent.
pad: your sketchboard — a free-form workspace in your system prompt (system/pad.md). Use it liberally.
context: your conversation context window.
psyche.action:
lingtai: update | load. update auto-loads.
pad: edit | load | append. edit auto-loads. append pins files as read-only reference.
context: molt. Requires `summary` — your charge to the next you. Tend the four stores (lingtai, pad, codex, library) BEFORE molting; the summary is the briefing on top, not a replacement.
psyche.content: Text content. For lingtai update: write your full identity profile — your character — who you are, what you know, how you work, what you care about, and what you have learned about yourself. This replaces the previous version entirely, so include everything. For pad edit: written as-is to pad.md. Don't be precious — rewrite freely, as often as you need.
psyche.files: File paths (text files only). For pad edit: each file's content is appended after your text content, separated by [file-1], [file-2], etc. For pad append: sets the list of files pinned as read-only reference in your system prompt — they are re-read and appended to your pad on every load (including after molt). Pin anything you want persistent visibility on: codex exports, source files you're editing, skill docs, config files, research notes — whatever helps you work. Pass files=[] to clear. Call append with no files param to see the current list. Total appended content must not exceed 100k tokens. Paths relative to working directory.
psyche.summary: For context molt: your charge to the self that comes after you — the only conversation-layer thing the next you will see. Write what you are working on, what you have accomplished, what remains, who to contact, which codex IDs to load (via `codex(read, ...)`), and which library SKILL.md paths the next task needs. Not a recap of conversation — a briefing for the next you. ~10,000 tokens. The four durable stores (lingtai, pad, codex, library) must be tended BEFORE molt; the summary does not replace them.
psyche.keep_tool_calls: Optional list of LingTai tool-call ids (e.g. "tc_1730330127_a3f1", visible in each tool result's _tool_call_id field) to preserve across the molt. Each named pair (tool_use + matching tool_result) is replayed into the fresh session right after the summary — useful for retaining a few high-value file reads, search results, or computations the next you should still see verbatim. If any id is not found, the molt is refused and the molt count is unchanged — retry with a corrected list. Do not abuse this: the four durable stores are still the primary persistence surface; keep this list short.
codex.description:
Knowledge archive — a persistent store for important findings, data, decisions, and discoveries. Persists across molts, reboots, and kills. There is an upper limit on entries — treat each slot as precious. Consolidate related entries regularly and use the supplementary field to pack extended detail into fewer entries. submit to add entries. filter to browse (returns id + title + summary). view to read full content. consolidate to merge entries. delete to remove. export to freeze entries as immutable text files — pair export with psyche(pad, edit, files=[...]) or psyche(pad, append, files=[...]) to load codex content into your pad.
PRIVACY: Codex IDs are private to you — never share them with peers. Other agents cannot access your codex. When you need to share knowledge, always share the full content via email or write it to a file and share the file path.
codex.action: submit: add a new entry (requires title, summary, content). filter: browse entries (optional pattern, limit). view: read full entries by ID (requires ids). consolidate: merge entries into one (requires ids, title, summary, content). delete: remove entries (requires ids). export: freeze entries as immutable text files (requires ids) — returns file paths for use in psyche(pad, edit, files=[...]).
codex.title: Entry title — one line. Required for submit and consolidate.
codex.summary: Entry summary — 1-3 sentences, used for filtering. Required for submit and consolidate.
codex.content: Main body text — up to 500 words. Required for submit and consolidate.
codex.supplementary: Extended material — unbounded. Optional for submit and consolidate. Use when the content alone doesn't capture full detail.
codex.ids: Entry IDs — for view, consolidate, delete, and export.
codex.pattern: Regex pattern for filter. Searches across titles, summaries, and content. Omit to list all entries.
codex.limit: Maximum entries to return for filter.
codex.depth: Depth for view. 'content' (default): id + title + summary + content. 'supplementary': includes supplementary field too.
avatar.description: Spawn a 他我 (alter ego) — a fully independent agent process born from you. The 他我 inherits your init.json (covenant, principle, language, etc.). For preset-equipped agents: avatar boots on your DEFAULT preset (manifest.preset.default), not your currently-active one — keeps the avatar's notion of 'home' stable as a peer in the network. Once spawned, it is a detached process — a new life that survives your death. Use mail or email to communicate. type='shallow' (default, 投胎): blank slate with only init.json inherited. type='deep' (二重身): copies your lingtai (identity), pad, codex, and knowledge — a doppelgänger. All spawns are recorded in delegates/ledger.jsonl — check the ledger before spawning again. IMPORTANT: The reasoning field becomes the avatar's starting prompt — write a thorough mission briefing. Actions: 'spawn' (default): create a new 他我. 'rules': set network-wide rules distributed to all descendants — requires admin privilege (karma). Caring for your avatars: After you spawn an avatar, please record it in your psyche pad — the address, the mission you gave it, and why you delegated this work. Your pad is the living roster of the delegations you are accountable for. When an avatar reports back or completes its task, update the entry. If an avatar goes quiet when you expected a reply, or your mail to it starts bouncing, please don't send probe mails to check on it — instead, report the situation upstream by emailing your own parent, who can decide whether to cpr the avatar, escalate further, or accept the loss. This way failures propagate up the delegation chain naturally, without anyone having to poll the network.
avatar.name: True name (真名) for the 他我 (required, e.g. 'researcher', 'analyst', '学者'). This doubles as the avatar's working-directory basename under .lingtai/, so it must be a bare single segment: letters (any script — Latin, CJK, Cyrillic, etc.), digits, underscore, or hyphen only. No slashes, no dots, no spaces, no leading '.', max 64 chars. The avatar's display name (nickname) can be set separately and has no such constraints.
avatar.type: Spawn type. 'shallow' (default, 投胎): blank slate — only init.json (LLM config + capabilities) inherited. 'deep' (二重身): full copy of character, pad, and codex.
avatar.comment: A persistent system-level note injected into the avatar's system prompt (rendered last, after memory). This is NOT inherited from parent — it defaults to empty. Use this ONLY for instructions the avatar must ALWAYS remember across all molts and context resets — e.g. critical constraints, environment setup notes, or safety rules. This survives everything: molt, refresh, sleep/wake. Leave empty unless you have something the avatar should never forget.
avatar.action: Action to perform. 'spawn' (default): create a new 他我. 'rules': set network rules — writes .rules to your directory and distributes to all descendants in the avatar tree. Requires admin privilege (karma). Rules are injected into the system prompt (after covenant, before tools) and persist across molts.
avatar.rules_content: Rules content (required for action='rules'). Plain text — one rule per line. These are non-negotiable constraints distributed to all your descendants. Example: 'Always report findings via email.\nDo not spawn more than 3 avatars.'
avatar.parent_prompt: [system] You are an avatar of {parent_name}, whose address is {parent_address}. Please keep this in your psyche memory so you remember who spawned you. When you complete your mission, encounter problems you cannot resolve, or need to report back, email your parent at the address above.
email.description:
Internal inter-agent email within your lingtai network — NOT real email over the internet. Messages reach other agents in your .lingtai/ network via the local filesystem. For real external email (Gmail, Outlook, etc.), use the imap tool if available. Addresses: internal email addresses are relative paths under .lingtai/, without @ signs. For example, the human operator is usually at 'human'. External email addresses (containing @, like alice@gmail.com) belong to the imap tool, not here. Reply-routing discipline: Always reply on the channel the message arrived on. Internal email in → internal email out, using the sender's address from the message's from field (a bare path, not an @ address). imap in → imap out. If you see 'No agent at X' from the email tool and X contains @, you passed an IMAP address to the wrong tool — fix the address, don't switch channels. A single person (especially a human) may reach you through both channels with two different addresses — one per channel. Prefer reply over send when responding: reply uses the original message's metadata and cannot cross address spaces. Never reply via text output — text output is your private diary only you can see, not a communication channel. Filesystem-based mailbox with inbox/sent/archive folders, reply, reply-all, CC/BCC, attachments, regex search, and contacts. Use 'send' for outgoing email (optional delay for scheduled delivery). 'check' to list inbox, sent, or archive (optional folder param). 'read' to read by ID. 'reply'/'reply_all' to respond. 'search' to find emails by regex (searches from, subject, message). 'archive' to move emails from inbox to archive. 'delete' to remove emails from inbox or archive. 'contacts' to list saved contacts. 'add_contact' to register a peer (address, name, optional note). 'remove_contact' to delete a contact by address. 'edit_contact' to update fields on an existing contact. Attachments are stored alongside emails in the mailbox. Every email carries the sender's identity card, surfaced directly in check/read results: sender_name (true name), sender_nickname (display name, may be empty), sender_agent_id (permanent birth ID — stable even after moves/renames), sender_language (their language), is_human (true = human operator, not AI), and sender_location (city, region, timezone — present for human operators, refreshed hourly). When you receive mail from a human, check sender_location to understand their timezone and locale — greet them appropriately for their local time, and schedule reminders in their timezone. When you receive mail, check the identity card and update your contacts promptly: if the address changed, update it; if they have a new nickname, note it. Use 'note' field in contacts to record agent_id so you can recognize the same agent even if they move or rename. IMPORTANT: Always address people by sender_nickname if available, otherwise by sender_name. This applies to both humans and agents. Never use raw addresses or agent_ids in conversation — use the person's name. Etiquette: a short acknowledgement is fine, but do not reply to an acknowledgement — that creates pointless ping-pong. Pass a 'schedule' object instead of 'action' for recurring sends. schedule.action='create': start recurring send (requires address, message, schedule.interval, schedule.count). schedule.action='cancel': pause a running schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). schedule.action='reactivate': resume a paused schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). schedule.action='list': show all schedules with status.
PRIVACY: Email IDs and schedule IDs are private to your mailbox — other agents cannot access your emails or schedules by ID. Never share these IDs with peers. To share information from an email, quote or forward the actual content. To share files, write them to disk and share the file path, or attach them to an outgoing email. Note: machine-readable timestamp metadata in returned email payloads (received_at, sent_at, deliver_at) is always UTC, regardless of the agent's timezone awareness setting.
email.action: send: send with optional cc/bcc (requires address, message). check: list mailbox with preview of each email (up to 500 chars). If truncated, the remaining char count is shown — use 'read' to see full content. (optional folder, n). read: read emails by ID list (email_id=[id1, id2, ...]). You are encouraged to read multiple relevant or even all unread emails and think before acting. reply: reply to email (requires email_id, message). reply_all: reply to all recipients (requires email_id, message). search: regex search mailbox (requires query, optional folder). archive: move email(s) from inbox to archive (requires email_id). delete: remove email(s) from inbox or archive (requires email_id, optional folder). contacts: list all contacts. add_contact: add/update contact (requires address, name; optional note). remove_contact: remove contact (requires address). edit_contact: update contact fields (requires address; optional name, note).
email.cc: CC addresses — visible to all recipients
email.bcc: BCC addresses — hidden from other recipients
email.subject: Email subject line
email.email_id: List of email IDs for read. For reply/reply_all, pass a single-element list.
email.n: Max recent emails to show (for check, default 10)
email.query: Regex pattern for search (matches from, subject, message)
email.folder: Folder for check/search/read/delete. Default: inbox for check, both for search. Note: 'sent' is read-only — delete only works on inbox or archive.
email.delay: Delay in seconds before delivery (default: 0). Use for scheduled or deferred sends.
email.filter: Optional filter object for check. Pass filter={sort, from, subject, contains, after, before, unread_only, has_attachments, truncate} to narrow and control results.
email.filter_contains: Filter by message body content (case-insensitive substring match).
email.filter_after: Only show emails after this ISO 8601 timestamp (e.g. 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z).
email.filter_before: Only show emails before this ISO 8601 timestamp.
email.filter_has_attachments: Only show emails that have attachments.
email.filter_truncate: Max characters for message preview (default 500). Set to 0 for full message body.
email.name: Contact's human-readable name (for add_contact, edit_contact)
email.note: Free-text note about the contact (for add_contact, edit_contact)
email.contact_address: The contact's address (working directory path). Optional.
email.schedule_action: create: start a recurring send (requires address, message + schedule.interval, schedule.count). cancel: pause a running schedule. With schedule_id, pauses that one. Without schedule_id, pauses ALL running schedules. reactivate: resume a paused schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). list: show all schedules with status.
vision.description: Analyze an image using the LLM's vision capabilities. Supports JPEG, PNG, and WebP. Ask any question about the image — describe contents, read text, interpret charts, identify objects, assess style or mood. Combine with draw to generate then analyze images.
vision.question: Question about the image
web_search.description: Search the web for current information. Use for real-time data, recent events, documentation, or anything beyond your training knowledge. Returns ranked search results with titles, URLs, and snippets. To fetch and read a specific URL, or for deeper strategies (PDF download, JS-rendered pages, scraping with stealth, fallback APIs), see the `web-browsing` skill.
daemon.description:
Daemon (神識) — delegate work to ephemeral subagents (emanations) for context isolation. Each emanation is a disposable LLM session with its own context window; it shares your working directory but retains NO memory after completion. Use daemon to keep noisy, context-heavy work out of your own context: large file scans, exploratory searches, multi-step research, batch transformations — anything where you only need the conclusion. Max 4 concurrent by default (configurable per agent in init.json; daemon(action='list') reports the actual cap). For tasks that need persistent memory or learning across sessions, use avatar (分身) instead — daemon is fire-and-forget. IMPORTANT: emanation results are truncated to ~2000 chars. If you need detailed output, instruct the emanation to write a report to a file, then read the file yourself. Actions: emanate (分, dispatch batch), list (观, check status), ask (问, follow-up), reclaim (收, kill all).
FILESYSTEM: Each emanation creates a forensic folder at daemons/<run_id>/ with daemon.json (live status), history/chat_history.jsonl (transcript), logs/{token_ledger,events}.jsonl. Read these instead of killing a slow emanation prematurely — see the daemon-manual skill for inspection patterns. Folders persist forever (cleaned up by molts).
PRIVACY: Emanation IDs (em-N) are private to your daemon context — other agents cannot use them. Never share emanation IDs with peers. If an emanation produces useful results, share the actual content via email or write it to a file.
daemon.action: Action to perform: 'emanate' (dispatch subagents), 'list' (show status), 'ask' (follow-up message), 'check' (read recent events of one emanation), 'reclaim' (kill all)
daemon.last: For 'check': how many most-recent events to return from events.jsonl. Default 20. Each turn produces ~2 events (tool_call + tool_result), so last=20 covers ~10 tool dispatches.
daemon.truncate: For 'check': maximum length of any string field in returned events; longer fields get an ellipsis suffix. Default 500. Set to 0 to disable.
daemon.max_turns: For 'emanate': max LLM tool-loop turns per emanation in this batch. Defaults to the agent's ceiling (200); your value is capped at the ceiling — you cannot raise it, only lower it. Use a smaller value to keep simple emanations bounded.
daemon.timeout: For 'emanate': max wall-clock seconds for the whole batch. Defaults to the agent's ceiling (3600s = 1 hour); your value is capped at the ceiling. Watchdog kills any emanation still running when the timer fires.
daemon.tasks: List of task objects for 'emanate'. Each: {task: str (required — instructions including where to save work), tools: list[str] (required — capability names, e.g. ['file', 'bash']), preset: str (optional — spawn this daemon on a different preset, e.g. preset='deepseek' for a cheap-thinking daemon, preset='minimax' for vision work). Omit preset to use the parent's currently-active tool surface (LLM + capabilities). When a preset is given, the emanation's tool surface comes from the preset's manifest.capabilities (instantiated fresh, so the preset can carry tools the parent does not have — e.g. a 'minimax' preset can give an emanation 'vision' even if the parent never set it up). MCP tools are always inherited from the parent. Inside preset capabilities, provider:'inherit' resolves against the preset's own LLM, not the parent's. The kernel validates the preset name against your library, instantiates its capabilities, and runs a connectivity check before spawning — unknown, broken, or unreachable presets refuse the WHOLE BATCH.
library.description: Your per-agent skill library. The <available_skills> catalog in your system prompt lists every skill reachable right now. Call info to read the full library workflow (authoring, publishing, loading via pad.append) and verify your library is healthy. See also the library-manual skill in .library/intrinsic/.
library.action_info: info: return the library-manual skill body plus a runtime health snapshot (catalog size, resolved paths, problems).
library.preamble: The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. When a task matches a skill's description, use the read tool to load the SKILL.md file at the path shown in that skill's location field, then follow its instructions. Each skill folder may contain supporting files (scripts, templates, data) — the SKILL.md will reference them. Skills you use often can be pinned into your pad via psyche({object:'pad', action:'append', files:[location]}) for cross-turn persistence. If no skill clearly applies, do not load any.
